skill.name: azure-eventhub-py
skill.description: Guidance for building Python producers, consumers, checkpointing flows, partition access, and async streaming with Azure Event Hubs.
skill.summary: Build Python streaming workflows with Azure Event Hubs producers, consumers, partitions, and checkpointing.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged markdown code fences as command execution and flagged a weak cryptography pattern on YAML description text. Manual review found no executable scripts, no prompt injection, and no weak cryptographic logic; the remaining risk is normal Azure network connectivity shown in SDK examples.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across SKILL.md. The reviewed locations are markdown code fences, tables, Python SDK examples, and inline formatting, not executable skill logic. The only shell content is a user-facing pip install example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged ranges are documentation blocks in SKILL.md and are not invoked by any script. No file in the reported structure provides runtime code that executes these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Match in Description Text
low_findings.description: The high severity weak cryptography static finding points to SKILL.md line 3, which is YAML front matter for a human-readable description. No cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic API use is present at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 starts a description field and does not contain cryptographic code. The surrounding front matter only names the skill and package.

low_findings.title: Azure Network Endpoint Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are placeholder Azure Blob Storage endpoint examples. They demonstrate expected Azure SDK configuration and do not send secrets to an unrelated endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URLs use placeholder account names under Azure Blob Storage. This is normal for Event Hubs checkpointing guidance, but it still indicates networked cloud service usage.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Event Hub Metadata Query Flagged as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance finding is a documented Azure SDK call that reads Event Hub partition properties from the configured namespace. It is administrative metadata access, not host or environment reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code calls producer.get_eventhub_properties and producer.get_partition_properties against Azure Event Hubs. It does not inspect the local system, environment variables, files, or process metadata.

content.user_title: Build Azure Event Hubs Python Streams
content.value_statement: Python teams need reliable patterns for Event Hubs producers, consumers, partitions, and checkpoints. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused Azure SDK guidance for streaming workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install Azure Event Hubs and Azure Identity Python packages.
Shows producer and consumer setup with DefaultAzureCredential.
Demonstrates event batching, partition IDs, and partition keys.
Describes BlobCheckpointStore usage for production checkpointing.
Includes async producer and consumer patterns for high-throughput workflows.
Shows how to read Event Hub and partition metadata.
limitations: Does not include executable helper scripts in the scanned file structure.
Does not provision Azure resources or assign Azure role permissions.
Uses placeholder namespaces, Event Hub names, and storage account URLs.
Does not cover every retry, monitoring, or disaster recovery option.

use_cases.title: Create a Streaming Producer
use_cases.description: Build a Python producer that batches events and sends them to Azure Event Hubs.

use_cases.title: Add Reliable Checkpointing
use_cases.description: Configure consumers with Blob Storage checkpoints so processing can resume after failures.

use_cases.title: Review Partition Behavior
use_cases.description: Understand partition keys, partition IDs, and metadata queries before designing event routing.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Producer
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-eventhub-py skill to explain how I should create a Python Event Hubs producer with DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Consumer
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-eventhub-py skill to outline a Python consumer that receives events and updates checkpoints safely.

prompt_templates.title: Design Partition Routing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-eventhub-py skill to compare partition IDs and partition keys for ordered event delivery in Python.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Async Streaming
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-eventhub-py skill to design an async Python Event Hubs workflow with producer, consumer, checkpoint store, and operational risks.

output_examples.input: Explain how to send ten events to Event Hubs from Python.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes installing the Azure packages, creating an authenticated producer, building batches, handling full batches, and sending remaining events.

output_examples.input: How should I consume events in production?
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends EventHubConsumerClient with BlobCheckpointStore, a dedicated consumer group, checkpoint updates after processing, and managed identity permissions.

output_examples.input: When should I use a partition key?
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that partition keys keep related events ordered within one partition, while direct partition IDs are useful for controlled routing.

best_practices: Use Azure managed identity or DefaultAzureCredential instead of connection strings when possible.
Checkpoint only after successful processing to avoid data loss.
Use batches and async clients for high-throughput workloads.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode real Azure credentials in prompts or source files.
Do not checkpoint before the event handler finishes required work.
Do not force all traffic into one partition unless ordering requires it.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The scanned skill file is documentation with examples. Users must run any package installs or Python code themselves.

faq.question: Does it support Azure managed identity?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples use DefaultAzureCredential, which can use managed identity in supported Azure environments.

faq.question: Can it create Event Hubs resources?
faq.answer: No. It explains SDK usage after the namespace, Event Hub, storage account, and permissions already exist.

faq.question: Does it cover checkpoint storage?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows BlobCheckpointStore configuration and explains checkpoint updates during event consumption.

faq.question: Can I use it for async Python applications?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes async producer and consumer patterns with the Azure Event Hubs async client.

faq.question: What security precautions should I take?
faq.answer: Use least-privilege Azure roles, avoid sharing real endpoint details in public prompts, and keep credentials outside source code.
